coredata.dc:title: Establishing carbon dots assisted high speed countercurrent chromatography and its application for efficient separation of rare earth element ions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Wenting
Wang, Wenjuan
Wei, Yun
coredata.dc:description: Rare earth elements with unique magnetic properties and optical properties, known as the ‘industrial vitamin’, has a very high commercial value. As a secondary resource of rare earth elements, low-concentration solution includes mixed rare earth ions, which need to realize efficient separation and recovery urgently. High speed countercurrent chromatography is suitable for the separation and purification of rare earth element ions due to its advantages of large loading, good tolerance to samples, and simple pretreatment. In this study, a carbon dots assisted high speed countercurrent chromatography method was designed and established, the carbon dots were applied to the mobile phase of high speed countercurrent chromatography for the first time. The low concentration of REEs solution was enriched, and the effective separation of La (III), Ce (III), Gd (III) and Er (III) was successfully achieved. The complete separation of La (III), Ce (III), Gd (III) and Er (III) was achieved with a solvent system of 0.05 mol L −1 P507 (PE), 0.05 mol L −1 HNO3, and 0.1 mol L −1 CDs2 carbon dots (1:1:0.01, v/v/v), the upper phase as stationary phase, the lower phase as mobile phase. Density functional theory result showed that the binding energy of REEs (III)-CDs2 was larger than that of REEs (III)-P507, so the affinity of CDs2 to REEs (III) was stronger than that of P507. Therefore, with the addition of CDs2, the ability of mobile phase to elute REEs from the stationary phase was enhanced, and the separation effect was improved.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rare earth elements
High speed countercurrent chromatography
Carbon dots
Separation
Density functional theory

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Theoretical calculation details for the mechanism of rare earth ions separation
Effect of carbon dots on HSCCC separation
Mechanism of separation of rare earth ions by carbon dots assisted HSCCC

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant No. 22074004).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HSCCC separation of rare earth ions.

(a) Solvent system 3: 0.15 mol L −1 P507 (PE)−0.1 mol L −1 HNO3 (v/v, 1:1); (b) Solvent system 5: 0.05 mol L −1 P507 (PE)−0.1 mol L −1 HNO3 (v/v, 1:1); (c) Solvent system 6: 0.05 mol L −1 P507 (PE)−0.05 mol L −1 HNO3 (v/v, 1:1); (d) Solvent system 6 with extrusion elution using N2: 0.05 mol L −1 P507 (PE)−0.05 mol L −1 HNO3 (v/v, 1:1).
HSCCC separation of rare earth ions.
Solvent system: 0.05 mol L −1 P507 (PE)−0.05 mol L −1 HNO3–CDs2 (v/v/v, 1:1:0.05) rotate speed: 800 r min−1; flow rate of mobile phase: 2 mL min−1.
HSCCC separation of rare earth ions.
Solvent system: 0.05 mol L −1 P507 (PE)−0.05 mol L −1 HNO3–CDs2 (v/v/v, 1:1:0.01) rotate speed: 800 r min−1; flow rate of mobile phase: 2 mL min−1.
(a) TEM image of CDs2; (b) FTIR image of CDs2.
Binding energy and optimized structures of complexes of rare earth element ions with P507 and CDs2 (folic acid as a basic unit).
The K value of four rare earth ions in different solvent systems.
Binding energy of REEs-CDs2 and REEs (OH)3 complexes.

head.ce:title: Establishing carbon dots assisted high speed countercurrent chromatography and its application for efficient separation of rare earth element ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rare earth elements with unique magnetic properties and optical properties, known as the ‘industrial vitamin’, has a very high commercial value. As a secondary resource of rare earth elements, low-concentration solution includes mixed rare earth ions, which need to realize efficient separation and recovery urgently. High speed countercurrent chromatography is suitable for the separation and purification of rare earth element ions due to its advantages of large loading, good tolerance to samples, and simple pretreatment. In this study, a carbon dots assisted high speed countercurrent chromatography method was designed and established, the carbon dots were applied to the mobile phase of high speed countercurrent chromatography for the first time. The low concentration of REEs solution was enriched, and the effective separation of La (III), Ce (III), Gd (III) and Er (III) was successfully achieved. The complete separation of La (III), Ce (III), Gd (III) and Er (III) was achieved with a solvent system of 0.05 mol L −1 P507 (PE), 0.05 mol L −1 HNO3, and 0.1 mol L −1 CDs2 carbon dots (1:1:0.01, v/v/v), the upper phase as stationary phase, the lower phase as mobile phase. Density functional theory result showed that the binding energy of REEs (III)-CDs2 was larger than that of REEs (III)-P507, so the affinity of CDs2 to REEs (III) was stronger than that of P507. Therefore, with the addition of CDs2, the ability of mobile phase to elute REEs from the stationary phase was enhanced, and the separation effect was improved.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rare earth elements (REEs) are widely used in various fields due to their special electronic structure, with high industrial and commercial value, and are known as ‘modern industrial vitamins’. Because single REEs plays an important role in many applications, including permanent magnet materials, hydrogen storage materials, high temperature superconductivity, optical glasses, laser crystals, etc. [1,2], separating and purifying single REEs is a challenging but essential task. At the same time, as a secondary resource of REEs, low-concentration solution includes mixed rare earth ions, which need to realize efficient separation and recovery urgently. REEs have the same extra-nuclear electron configuration except for the number of 4f electrons, and there is a phenomenon of ‘lanthanide contraction’ [3]. Therefore, REEs are mostly stable in aqueous solution in the form of trivalent states, which have a high affinity with water, and are protected by hydrates, and their chemical properties are very similar, making separation and purification extremely difficult. As the mainstream separation method in the field of REEs, traditional solvent extraction method usually requires a large number of organic solvent and is prone to excessive emulsification of organic phase. Not only the utilization rate of resources is reduced, but also it pollutes the ecological environment. Therefore, it is of great significance to seek an efficient, green and easy to operate separation technology.

High speed countercurrent chromatography (HSCCC) has the advantages of large loading capacity, good tolerance to samples and simple pretreatment, which is suitable for the separation and purification of REEs. However, there are still some problems such as low separation efficiency and long separation time [4-12]. Therefore, as a new method for the separation and purification of REEs, HSCCC is still in the development stage, further research and optimization are still needed.

As a type of nanomaterials, carbon dots have unique optical and electrical properties, good water solubility and biocompatibility, low toxicity, simple preparation method, environment friendly, and abundant low-cost sources. The surface of most carbon dots has hydrophilic groups such as carboxyl group and amino group, and makes the high surface charge density of carbon dots, which can form chelation and electrostatic attraction with REEs, so CDs are favorable for adsorption of REEs. Therefore, carbon dots were added to HSCCC, the distribution ratio of REEs in the two-phase solvent would be effectively improved, the separation efficiency of REEs could be increased, and the separation time could be shortened, so that there are the great potential of carbon dots in the application of HSCCC.

In this study, a carbon dots assisted HSCCC method was designed and established for the separation of REEs. Four kinds of mixed REEs enriched by solvent flotation from modeling water sample according to our previous report [13] were selected as the target, and the basic solvent system was determined by selecting the appropriate solvent system and optimizing the ratio of each solvent according to a general ‘golden rule’ for choosing the suitable two-phase solvent system [14,15]. The carbon dots were used as the solvent improver to optimize the selected basic solvent system, and the influence of the type and amount of carbon dots on the separation effect was investigated.

Melamine, sodium citrate, folic acid, citric acid, diethylenetriamine and urea used in the preparation of carbon dots were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., LTD. Sodium hydroxide, hydrochloric acid and petroleum ether used in the previous solvent flotation process were purchased from Beijing Chemical Plant, while 2-ethylhexyl phosphate (P507) was purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. La (III), Ce (III), Gd (III), Er (III) hexahydrate nitrate solution and standard solution were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., LTD.

Rare earth ions were separated by a semi-preparative high speed countercurrent chromatograph (HSCCC, EMC-360, Beijing Emelin Technology, China). The HSCCC isolation was performed on a multilayer coil planet centrifuge (Beijing Emelin technology Co., LTD) equipped with a polytetrafluoroethylene multilayer coil (1.6 mm i.d., 320 mL total volume) and a 10 mL sample loop. The β value of the coil was 0.5 at the internal terminal while 0.75 at the external terminal (β = r/R, where r is the distance from the holder shaft to the coil, and R is the distance from the holder axis to the central axis of the planet centrifuge or the rotation radius). The revolution speed was 800 r min−1 in the present study. Column was filled with solvent by using pump of invariable flow (Model NS-1007) equipped by Beijing Institute of New Technology Application. The concentrations of all metal ion solutions were determined by Inductively Coupled Plasma Optical Emission Spectrometer (ICP-OES, Perkin Elmer-8000, PerkinElmer, USA). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a Tacni G2 F20 Field emission Transmission Electron Microscope (FEI., USA) at an accelerated voltage of 100 kV. The Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet 8700) in the 400–4000 cm−1 vibration region was used to characterize the functional groups of carbon dots.
In this study, four kinds of carbon dots were prepared to assist high speed countercurrent chromatography method and improve the distribution coefficient of the solvent system.

Carbon dots 1 (CDs1): 0.768 g melamine and 1.870 g sodium citrate were accurately weighed, dissolved in 80 mL deionized water and ultrasonically dissolved for 5 min. After reaction at 180 °C for 12 h by hydrothermal method, 0.22 μm filter membrane was applied and stored in refrigerator at 4 °C.

Carbon dots 2 (CDs2): 100 mg folic acid was dissolved in 100 mL water and ultrasonically dissolved for 2 min. After reaction at 180 °C by hydrothermal method for 6 h, it was taken out and filtered through 0.22 μm filter membrane, and then stored in refrigerator at 4 °C.

Carbon dots 3 (CDs3): 2.0 g of citric acid was accurately weighed and dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. 1 mL of diethylenetriamine was added to the solution and ultrasonically dissolved for 2 min. The solution was heated by microwave at 700 W for 7 min and then removed. 10 mL of deionized water was immediately added and then passed through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

A 1.73 mL of 95 % P507 was measured and filled into a 100 mL volumetric bottle, which was diluted with petroleum ether to obtain 0.05 mol L −1 P507 (PE) solution. A 3.13 mL of 16 mol L −1 nitric acid was measured and filled into 1000 mL volumetric bottle, which was diluted with deionized water to obtain 0.05 mol L −1 HNO3 solution.

Amount of 311.7 mg of La (NO3)3⋅6H2O, 309.9 mg of Ce (NO3)3⋅6H2O, 287.0 mg of Gd (NO3)3⋅6H2O and 275.8 mg of Er (NO3)3⋅6H2O were weighed and dissolved in deionized water in a 1000 mL volumetric bottle to prepare a mixed solution (100 mg L −1) of La (III), Ce (III), Gd (III) and Er (III), which was sealed and stored at 4 °C.

The organic phase including four REEs was collected, which was obtained by solvent flotation in our previous study [13], the petroleum ether was removed at 60 °C and the oil was collected in a sample bottle and sealed at 4 °C.

The selection of suitable two-phase solvent system is the key to the successful separation of target components by high speed countercurrent chromatography. There is usually a general ‘golden rule’ [14,15]: (1) the target compound can be fully dissolved in a two-phase solvent, and the physicochemical properties of the sample do not change; (2) The layering time of the two-phase solvent should be less than 30 s, and the volume ratio of the two-phase solvent should be approximately 1 in order to reduce solvent waste; (3) The stationary phase of the solvent system must be retained enough in the separation column, usually the retention rate is more than 40 %; (4) The distribution coefficient of the target compound should be in the range of 0.2 < K < 5, and the resolution between any two components (α = K2 /K1, K2 >K1 ) should be greater than 1.5. Therefore, the suitable solvent system is mainly selected by determining the distribution coefficient (K) of the components to be separated in each solvent system.

The K value was determined in this experiment as follows: 15 mL of the lower phase solvent was taken into a 25 mL sample bottle and 200 μL of mixed REEs solution (100 mg L −1) was added to the bottle. After vigorous shaking to make it evenly dispersed, 5 mL of lower phase was accurately fetched into a 10 mL sample bottle for ICP-OES analysis. The concentration of REEs was denoted C1 . And then another 10 mL of upper phase was added to shake thoroughly and stood for stratification for 30 min. After equilibration, another 5 mL of lower phase was aspirated and the concentration was detected by ICP-OES. The concentration of REEs was denoted C2 . Then the distribution coefficient of rare earth ions in the two-phase solvent system can be calculated by the following Eq. (1):
(1)
K =
(
C 1
−
C 2
)
C 2
where C1 is the first lower phase concentration, C2 is the second lower phase concentration.

At room temperature, the two phase solvent systems obtained by screening were prepared respectively according to the volume ratio in a 2 L separation funnel, and were fully oscillated and mixed, and left for 30 min. After the two phases were balanced, the upper and lower phases were separated in different solvent bottles. The P507 organic phase was selected as the stationary phase, and the low-concentration aqueous nitric acid phase was used as the mobile phase and degassed by ultrasonic for 5 min before use. 2 mL of rare earth ion enrichment solution was accurately added to 25 mL sample bottles, 5 mL of upper and lower phase solution was added respectively, and fully shaken and mixed as injection solution for later use.
The HSCCC separation process is as follows:

The stationary phase was pumped into the multi-layer spiral string with a constant flow pump at the flow rate of 9 mL min−1 until the volume of the outgoing stationary phase was greater than 200 mL to ensure that the multi-layer spiral string was filled with the stationary phase. After that, the flow rate was adjusted to 2 mL min−1, the countercurrent chromatograph was started at a revolution speed of 800 r min−1, and 2 mL sample solution was injected. The head to tail elution mode (descending mode) was used. In the process of pumping the mobile phase, a certain amount of stationary phase would be eluted, and the effluent would be collected and observed for stratification. When the outflow was stably stratified and the volume of the upper phase remained unchanged, it was proved that the upper and lower phases in the column had reached hydrodynamic equilibrium. The volume of the upper phase was recorded and the retention rate of the stationary phase was calculated. Subsequently, the sample solution was injected into the sample ring, and the effluent was collected into the test tube every 5 min. The resulting fractions were detected by ICP-OES.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical calculation details for the mechanism of rare earth ions separation
ce:section.ce:para:
The Gaussain 09, Revision D.01 software package was used to carry out density functional theory (DFT) calculation. The energy of the ligand and REEs (III) and the energy of the complex were calculated respectively, and the corresponding binding energy was calculated. The affinity of CDs2 and P507 for REEs (III) was evaluated based on the binding energy of the complexes. Specifically, the binding energy of the complexes were estimated by the total energy of the complex minus the sum of the total energy of the ligand and REEs, as shown in Eq. (2):
(2)
Δ
E
= E
( complex )
−
[
E
( ligand )
+ E
(
REE
( III )
)
]
where E is the total energy of the complex (eV); ΔE is the binding energy of the complex (eV).

The structure optimization and energy calculation were done by the B3LYP hybrid basis set, the basis group of 6–31G** for C, H, O, N, P atoms and SDD for La (III), Ce (III), Gd (III), Er (III) atoms were applied.

From the previous flotation experiment results [13], it was seen that P507 had a strong separation ability for light and heavy rare earth ions, so P507 was selected as the organic phase of HSCCC for separation. Chloroform or benzene were usually used as diluent in common solvent system for P507 separation of rare earth ions [16,17]. Considering the strong toxicity of chloroform, it can cause acute poisoning by inhalation, and chloroform is also harmful to the environment. Therefore, dichloromethane and petroleum ether were chosen as diluents. P507 was used as the stationary phase of HSCCC, and the effects of different diluents, different concentrations of stationary phase and different acidity of mobile phase on distribution coefficient were investigated (Shown in Table 1 ).

The results showed that the distribution coefficients of Gd (III) and Er (III) were both large in solvent system of 1–6, which meant that Gd (III) and Er (III) were strongly retained in the stationary phase and were not favorable to elution. The reason was that when the concentration of stationary phase and the acidity of mobile phase were fixed, the extraction distribution ratio of P507 depended on the equilibrium constant of complex extraction between P507 and rare earth ions. When other conditions were fixed, the extraction equilibrium constant was determined by the ionic radius and charge of rare earth ions. When the valence state of the extracted rare earth ion was constant, the smaller the radius of the rare earth ion, the more stable the complex formed, and the larger the distribution ratio was. The excessive distribution coefficient indicated that the solubility of the substance to be separated was high in the organic phase and low in the water phase. This made the substance strongly retained in the stationary phase, resulting in a large amount of solvent consumption. To some extent, it would also cause the response value of the chromatographic peak to decrease, the resolution was reduced, and the separation effect was worse.

The K values of light rare earth ions La (III) and Ce (III) were low in solvent systems of 3, 5 and 6, but within a reasonable and adjustable range. As seen from the results in Table 1, the distribution coefficients of four rare earth ions were effectively decreased by reducing the concentration of P507 in the organic phase. Moreover, with the decrease of acidity of water phase, the retention of rare earth ions in water phase became worse, and the distribution coefficient was increased slightly. Considering the distribution coefficients of four kinds of REEs in solvent system, solvent system of 3, 5 and 6 were selected for further study of HSCCC separation.

The results were shown in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1(a) showed that solvent system 3 did not achieve effective separation of La (III) and Ce (III), and due to the excessive distribution coefficients of Gd (III) and Er (III), the corresponding fractions were not detected within 6 h, resulting in a large amount of solvent consumption, which was not suitable for the separation of REEs. The concentration of P507 in the stationary phase was adjusted and solvent system 5 was selected for separation. The results were shown in Fig. 1(b). The separation of La (III) and Ce (III) was improved, but complete separation was still not achieved, and the Gd (III) and Er (III) still were not eluted with the mobile phase within 6 h. On this basis, the acidity of the mobile phase in the solvent system was adjusted to carry out the separation experiment, and the results were shown in Fig. 1(c). La (III) and Ce (III) were separated in a relatively short time, but the mobile phase with low acidity could not elute Gd (III) and Er (III) retained in the stationary phase. Therefore, after the La (III) and Ce (III) fractions were collected within 180 min, the pump was turned off, and N2 was introduced to blow out the fractions at a flow rate of 2 mL min−1. As shown in Fig. 1(d), Gd (III) and Er (III) in the blown fraction were also resolved well, so solvent system 6 was selected to further optimize the HSCCC separation conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of carbon dots on HSCCC separation
ce:section.ce:para: The surface of most carbon dots was mainly modified by hydrophilic groups such as carboxyl group and amino group, which made its strong hydrophilicity and the large charge density of the surface of carbon dots. Among them, the electronegativity of the surface of the nitrogen-doped carbon dots was enhanced by introducing amino groups, which made it easy to combine with the REEs to be separated. Therefore, the distribution of REEs in the stationary phase and the mobile phase was improved, and the separation effect of countercurrent chromatography was further optimized. The influence of three kinds of hydrophilic nitrogen doped carbon dots as improver of two-phase solvent system on the separation effect of countercurrent chromatography was investigated. The results of distribution coefficient determination were shown in Table 1.

In system 7, La (III) and Ce (III) were hardly retained in the stationary phase, which was caused by the addition of CDs1. As a result, the sample was quickly eluted from the countercurrent chromatography system, and the separation efficiency was seriously reduced without adequate distribution. The K value of Er (III) in system 9 was large, and the sample was strongly retained in the stationary phase and difficult to be eluted by the mobile phase. In addition, The K value resolution of La (III) and Ce (III) in system 9 was less. Therefore, system 8 was selected for HSCCC separation.

As shown in Fig. 2 , all the four REEs were separated well by the addition of CDs2, but the separation degree of La (III) and Ce (III) became worse during the separation process. In fact, for the P507-HNO3 system, the process of mobile phase elution of REEs in HSCCC was the reverse extraction of REEs from P507. The reverse extraction of heavy REEs from P507 needed to be carried out at a higher acidity. It took a longer time to reach the extraction equilibrium because the extraction rate of heavy REEs was slow, so the light REEs were easier to be eluted at a lower acidity. The surface of CDs2 was rich in a large number of nitrogen-containing groups (amino groups) and oxygen-containing functional groups (hydroxyl and carbonyl groups), which made CDs2 easy to combine with REEs [18]. Therefore, with the addition of CDs2, the elution ability of mobile phase was enhanced, which made the light REEs Ce (III) and La (III) with good separation degree elute together, and the separation effect became worse.

In order to further realize the complete separation of four REEs, the addition of CDs2 was reduced based on system 8, and HSCCC was performed for separation. The results were shown in Fig. 3 . The baseline separation of four REEs was achieved within 4 h. Therefore, 0.05 mol L −1 P507 (PE) −0.05 mol L −1 HNO3–CDs2 (1:1:0.01, v/v/v) system was selected as the solvent system for the separation of REEs by HSCCC.

The separation results of HSCCC showed that the actual outflow time of each REEs was not consistent with the theoretical predicted value [19]. In general, the retention value of the organic component to be separated in the column can be predicted by Eq. (3):
(3)
V R
=
V M
+ K
V S
where VR is the retention volume of the component in the column, VM is the mobile phase volume, K is the distribution ratio of the component in the two phases, and VS is the stationary phase volume.

If the VM, VS , and K values of the component are known, the VR of the component can be accurately predicted. However, unlike the separation of organic substances, the separation of metal ions by HSCCC is a dynamic process [9], so the retention volume of components in the chromatographic column is not only dependent on the static distribution ratio, but also affected by the extraction reaction rate of metal ions in the two-phase transfer process. Therefore, the retention volume cannot be accurately predicted by the static distribution ratio, mobile phase volume and stationary phase volume [20].
In addition, in the process of HSCCC separation, it is advantageous to select the solvent system that can extract metal ions quickly to improve the separation efficiency.

The low acidity nitric acid solution is selected as the mobile phase, and the extraction of REEs by P507 under low acidity is a simple cation exchange mechanism. The extraction process can be expressed by Eq. (4):
(4)
RE
( o )
3 +
+ 3
H 2
A 2
⇌ RE
A 3
· 3 H
A
( o )
+ 3
H
+
where H2A2 is the dimer of P507, and REA3·3HA(o) is the complex in the organic phase at extraction equilibrium.

The extraction equilibrium constant K is calculated as shown in Eq. (5):
(5)
K =
[ RE
A
3
· 3
( HA )
]
(
o
)
[
H
+
]
3
[
R
E
3 +
]
[
H
2
A
2
]
3

Zhang et al. [21] used small centrifugal extraction equipment to study the extraction efficiency of REEs. It was found that when the aqueous phase equilibrium pH value was in the range of 1.69–2.19, the separation degree of centrifugal extraction was increased with the increase of aqueous phase equilibrium pH value, which was more conducive to separation. Therefore, when 0.05 mol L −1 HNO3 was used as the mobile phase, the separation of light REEs La (III) and Ce (III) could be achieved by solvent system 6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of separation of rare earth ions by carbon dots assisted HSCCC
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs2 was selected as the improver and characterized by TEM and FTIR [22]. The results were shown in Fig. 4 . As shown in Fig. 4(a), CDs2 was spherically dispersed with a particle size of 2–6 nm. As shown in Fig. 4(b), the absorption peaks at 3407 cm−1 and 3153 cm−1 were attributed to the antisymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of NH in the amide group, respectively, and the absorption peak at 1670 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of C = O in the amide group. The broad absorption peak at 2500 cm−1 – 3600 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of OH in carboxyl group, and the absorption peak at 1410 cm−1 was attributed to the bending vibration of OH in alcohol hydroxyl group. The FTIR image showed that, the surface of CDs2 was modified by a large number of nitrogen-containing groups and oxygen-containing groups. Since amino, hydroxyl and carbonyl groups were electron-rich groups, the surface charge density of CDs2 was relatively high. The higher charge density made it easier for CDs2 to combine with REEs to form new compounds that were more stable. The ability of the mobile phase to elute REEs from the stationary phase was enhanced, which greatly improved the retention of heavy REEs Gd (III) and Er (III) in the column.

The mechanism of microcosmic separation of REEs by carbon dots assisted HSCCC was studied using DFT calculation. The binding energy of REEs with stationary phase P507 and carbon dots CDs2 complexes was calculated respectively, and the results were shown in Fig. 5 . The chemical formula in the Fig. 5 was the structure of the complex optimized by DFT calculation.

The greater the absolute value of binding energy of the complexes, the stronger the interaction force between REEs (III) and the ligand, the more stable the complexes formed [23]. The results showed that, compared with P507, the absolute value of binding energies of CDs2 and REEs (III) was higher than that of P507 and corresponding REEs (III), which confirmed the strong affinity of CDs2 to REEs (III). Therefore, after the addition of CDs2, the ability of mobile phase to elute REEs from the stationary phase was enhanced, which greatly improved the retention of heavy REEs Gd (III) and Er (III) in the chromatographic column, and the separation effect was well improved.

Further, the binding energy of REEs (OH)3 was calculated, and the results were shown in Table 2 . It was found that the absolute value of binding energy of REEs (OH)3 was larger than that of CDs2 complex. This meant that separated REEs were able to recover by alkali precipitation.

In this study, a carbon dots assisted high speed countercurrent chromatography method was designed and established to separate La (III), Ce (III), Gd (III) and Er (III) from low concentration REEs solution enriched by solvent flotation. The basic solvent system was determined to be 0.05 mol L −1 P507 (PE) −0.05 mol L −1 HNO3 (1:1, v/v). Then the basic solvent system was optimized by using carbon dots as solvent improver, and the type and amount of carbon dots on the separation effect was investigated. Finally, the carbon dots system of 0.05 mol L −1 P507 (PE) −0.05 mol L −1 HNO3–CDs2 (1:1:0.01, v/v/v) was used for the recovery and separation of REEs. The separation of La (III), Ce (III), Gd (III) and Er (III) was successfully realized. The DFT calculation results showed that the affinity of CDs2 to REEs (III) was stronger than that of P507. Therefore, with the addition of CDs2, the ability of mobile phase to elute REEs from the stationary phase was enhanced, which improved the elution of heavy REEs Gd (III) and Er (III), and the separation effect was better. In addition, the separated REEs could be recovered well by alkali precipitation method according to DFT calculation. It is promising that carbon dots assisted high speed countercurrent chromatography method for separation of La (III), Ce (III), Gd (III) and Er (III), and also might be used for the separation of other REEs.

Wenting Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Wenjuan Wang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation. Yun Wei: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant No. 22074004).
